rules.description: Container is running in privileged mode
rules.risk: Grants full access to all host devices and disables most kernel isolation. An attacker inside the container can trivially escape to the host.
rules.remediation: Remove --privileged flag. Use --cap-add to grant only the specific capabilities required.

rules.description: Container launched by a systemd service without a non-root User directive
rules.risk: The container daemon process runs as UID 0 on the host. Combined with a container escape, this yields immediate root access to the host.
rules.remediation: Add 'User=<non-root>' to the systemd service unit file that starts the Docker daemon or container.

rules.description: Readonly root filesystem is not enabled
rules.risk: An attacker who gains code execution inside the container can write to the root filesystem, enabling persistence or binary replacement.
rules.remediation: Add --read-only flag when starting the container. Use tmpfs or volume mounts for writable paths.

rules.description: At least one bind mount is not readonly
rules.risk: Writable bind mounts allow the container to modify host files. An attacker can tamper with host binaries, cron jobs, or configuration files.
rules.remediation: Append ':ro' to bind mount specifications (e.g., -v /host/path:/container/path:ro) unless write access is strictly required.

rules.description: Seccomp profile is explicitly disabled (seccomp=unconfined)
rules.risk: Without a seccomp filter, the container process can invoke any syscall the kernel allows, expanding the attack surface for kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Remove '--security-opt seccomp=unconfined'. Use the default seccomp profile or provide a custom profile that restricts unnecessary syscalls.

rules.description: Container shares the host's process namespace
rules.risk: Sharing the host PID namespace allows the container to see and interact with all host processes, enabling process injection and information disclosure.
rules.remediation: Remove --pid=host flag. Do not set PidMode to 'host' unless strictly required for monitoring purposes.

rules.description: Container shares the host's network namespace
rules.risk: Sharing the host network namespace gives the container full access to host network interfaces and services bound to localhost, bypassing network isolation.
rules.remediation: Remove --network=host flag. Use bridge or overlay networking with explicit port mappings.

rules.description: Container shares the host's IPC namespace
rules.risk: Sharing the host IPC namespace allows the container to read and write shared memory segments of host processes, enabling data exfiltration or tampering.
rules.remediation: Remove --ipc=host flag. Use private IPC namespace (the default) unless inter-process communication with host processes is strictly required.

rules.description: Container has dangerous kernel capabilities added
rules.risk: Dangerous capabilities such as SYS_ADMIN, SYS_PTRACE, SYS_MODULE, NET_RAW, or NET_ADMIN greatly expand the container's attack surface, enabling kernel module loading, process tracing, and network-level attacks.
rules.remediation: Remove dangerous capabilities from --cap-add. Grant only the minimum capabilities required by the workload.

rules.description: No capabilities explicitly dropped
rules.risk: Without explicitly dropping unnecessary capabilities, the container retains the default capability set, which includes capabilities that may not be required and could be abused in an attack.
rules.remediation: Use --cap-drop=ALL and then --cap-add only the specific capabilities required by the workload.

rules.description: AppArmor profile is set to unconfined
rules.risk: Disabling AppArmor removes mandatory access control enforcement, allowing the container process unrestricted access to system resources within its namespace.
rules.remediation: Remove '--security-opt apparmor=unconfined'. Use the default AppArmor profile or provide a custom profile tailored to the workload.

rules.description: Container mounts a dangerous host path
rules.risk: Mounting sensitive host paths (/, /proc, /sys, /dev, /etc, docker.sock) breaks container isolation and enables container escape, credential theft, or full host compromise.
rules.remediation: Remove bind mounts for sensitive paths. Use named volumes or tmpfs instead.

rules.description: no-new-privileges security option is not set
rules.risk: Without no-new-privileges, processes inside the container can gain more privileges than their parent via setuid binaries or kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Add '--security-opt no-new-privileges' when starting the container.

rules.description: Bind mount uses shared or rshared propagation
rules.risk: Shared mount propagation allows mounts created inside the container to propagate to the host filesystem. An attacker can mount filesystems on the host from within the container, enabling container escape.
rules.remediation: Remove shared/rshared propagation from bind mounts. Use private or slave propagation instead.

rules.description: Container image uses :latest tag or has no explicit tag
rules.risk: Without a pinned image tag or digest, the container may run a different image version on each restart. An attacker who compromises the registry can push a malicious image with the same tag.
rules.remediation: Use a specific image tag and ideally a digest (e.g., image:v1.2.3@sha256:abc...).

rules.description: Container process runs as root (Config.User is empty, 'root', or '0')
rules.risk: Processes inside the container execute as UID 0. Combined with a container escape, this grants immediate host root access in rootful mode.
rules.remediation: Set a non-root user in the Dockerfile (USER directive) or via --user flag at container creation.

rules.description: No memory limit configured for the container
rules.risk: Without a memory limit, a runaway process can consume all host memory, triggering the OOM killer against arbitrary host processes including other containers.
rules.remediation: Set a memory limit via --memory flag or HostConfig.Memory field.

rules.description: No PID limit configured for the container
rules.risk: Without a PID limit, a fork bomb inside the container can exhaust the host PID table, causing a system-wide denial of service.
rules.remediation: Set a PID limit via --pids-limit flag or HostConfig.PidsLimit field.

rules.description: Container restart policy is set to 'always' or 'unless-stopped'
rules.risk: A compromised container with an always-restart policy will automatically recover after crashes, providing persistence for an attacker. Combined with privileged access, this enables sustained exploitation.
rules.remediation: Use 'on-failure' with a maximum retry count instead of 'always'. In systemd-managed environments, rely on systemd restart policies.

rules.description: Container logging is disabled (LogConfig.Type = none)
rules.risk: Disabled logging removes evidence of container activity. An attacker's actions inside the container will leave no audit trail, making incident response and forensic analysis impossible.
rules.remediation: Use a logging driver (json-file, journald, syslog) with appropriate size limits.

rules.description: Docker daemon does not enable user namespace remapping (userns-remap)
rules.risk: Without userns-remap, container UID 0 maps directly to host UID 0. Any container escape grants immediate root access to the host.
rules.remediation: Add '"userns-remap": "default"' to /etc/docker/daemon.json to enable user namespace isolation.

rules.description: Docker daemon allows inter-container communication (icc not disabled)
rules.risk: With icc enabled (default), any container can communicate with any other container on the same bridge network without explicit links or network policies. An attacker who compromises one container can scan and attack others.
rules.remediation: Add '"icc": false' to /etc/docker/daemon.json and use explicit --link or user-defined networks for inter-container communication.

rules.description: Device cgroup rules grant unrestricted device access
rules.risk: Broad device cgroup rules (e.g., 'a *:* rwm') allow the container to access all host character and block devices, including /dev/mem and raw disk. This enables direct host memory reading and container escape.
rules.remediation: Remove broad device cgroup rules. Grant access only to specific devices required by the workload.

rules.description: Container has direct access to dangerous host devices (/dev/mem, /dev/kmem, /dev/fuse, etc.)
rules.risk: Direct access to /dev/mem enables reading host kernel memory; /dev/fuse allows arbitrary filesystem mounting; /dev/sda grants raw disk access. These are direct container escape vectors.
rules.remediation: Remove dangerous device mappings from the container configuration. Use specific, non-privileged devices only.

rules.description: Container engine socket mounted writable (docker.sock/containerd.sock/crio.sock)
rules.risk: A writable engine socket grants full control over the container engine. An attacker can create privileged containers, access host filesystem, and achieve complete host compromise.
rules.remediation: Remove the socket mount or add ':ro' to make it read-only. Use socket proxies with restricted API access if needed.

rules.description: Container has custom /etc/hosts entries via ExtraHosts
rules.risk: Custom host entries can redirect traffic to attacker-controlled addresses for service impersonation or DNS rebinding within the container.
rules.remediation: Review ExtraHosts entries. Remove unless required for the workload. Use DNS-based service discovery instead.

rules.description: Container has excessively high nofile ulimit (>1M file descriptors)
rules.risk: Excessively high file descriptor limits can lead to file descriptor exhaustion attacks affecting the host and other containers.
rules.remediation: Set nofile ulimits to a reasonable value for the workload (typically 65536 or less).

rules.description: SELinux label uses super-privileged container type (spc_t)
rules.risk: The spc_t SELinux type bypasses all mandatory access control. The container has unrestricted access to host resources as if SELinux were disabled.
rules.remediation: Use a confined SELinux type. Remove 'label=type:spc_t' from security options.

rules.description: Container has no corresponding systemd service for automatic lifecycle management
rules.risk: In embedded/automotive environments all container services must be managed by systemd. A container without a systemd service will not auto-start at boot and has no supervised restart behavior.
rules.remediation: Create a systemd service unit file to manage the Docker container lifecycle (start, stop, restart).

rules.description: Systemd service does not restrict capabilities via CapabilityBoundingSet
rules.risk: Without CapabilityBoundingSet in the systemd unit, the Docker daemon inherits all capabilities from its parent process, increasing the blast radius if the daemon is compromised.
rules.remediation: Add 'CapabilityBoundingSet=' to the [Service] section of the systemd unit to restrict the daemon to only the capabilities it requires.
